question: Explain the primary purpose of the OSPF Hello protocol. What two primary functions does it serve in maintaining OSPF neighbor adjacencies?
expectedPoints: To discover OSPF neighbors on the same network segment.
To establish and maintain bidirectional communication between potential neighbors.
To serve as a keep-alive mechanism to detect a neighbor failure if Hellos are not received within the Dead Timer interval.

question: Describe the process and purpose of the OSPF Database Synchronization process between two routers forming an adjacency. Include the names of the key OSPF packet types involved in this process.
expectedPoints: The process ensures both routers have identical Link-State Databases (LSDBs) for the same area.
It begins with the exchange of Database Description (DBD) packets, which summarize the router's LSDB.
This is followed by Link-State Request (LSR) packets, where a router requests more details for LSAs it is missing or that are outdated.
The neighboring router responds with Link-State Update (LSU) packets containing the full LSA information.
Finally, Link-State Acknowledgment (LSAck) packets are used to confirm receipt of each LSU, ensuring reliable flooding.

question: A network administrator is designing a multi-area OSPF network for a large organization. Compare and contrast the roles and functions of an Area Border Router (ABR) and an Autonomous System Boundary Router (ASBR). Your answer must detail the types of LSAs each generates and their purpose in the OSPF hierarchy.
expectedPoints: An ABR connects one or more OSPF areas to the backbone area (Area 0). Its primary function is to summarize routes between areas and maintain separate LSDBs for each area it is connected to.
An ASBR connects the OSPF routing domain to an external non-OSPF domain (e.g., EIGRP, BGP, static routes). It is responsible for injecting external routes into the OSPF domain.
An ABR generates Type 3 (Summary) LSAs to advertise internal networks from one area into another area, and Type 4 (ASBR Summary) LSAs to advertise the location of an ASBR.
An ASBR generates Type 5 (External) LSAs to advertise routes learned from external routing protocols. These LSAs are flooded throughout the entire OSPF Autonomous System.
The key difference is that an ABR operates on area boundaries within a single OSPF AS, while an ASBR operates on the boundary between the OSPF AS and another routing domain.
